title: Mount/Add container unix socket files into the host (or named pipe)
body:
### Feature request description

when looking at some Docker Desktop backend extensions, these extensions are able to specify a unix socket that will use by the 'frontend' part

As we can't use directly sockets from a VM, the engine is adding on the host a socket that wraps the calls to the VM socket.

This is done as some people can't open ports on their computers and also because if you open a port, you can have conflicts with existing applications or future applications that will want to use that port number.

https://docs.docker.com/desktop/extensions-sdk/architecture/
> To communicate with the backend, the Extension SDK provides [functions](https://docs.docker.com/desktop/extensions-sdk/dev/api/backend/#get) to make GET, POST, PUT, HEAD, and DELETE requests from the frontend. Under the hood, the communication is done through a socket or named pipe, depending on the operating system. If the backend was listening to a port, it would be difficult to prevent collision with other applications running on the host or in a container already. Also, some users are running Docker Desktop in constrained environments where they can’t open ports on their machines.

### Suggest potential solution

Have a way to instruct podman machine to mount/add a host socket/named pipe redirecting traffic to the container running inside the VM (macOS/Windows case)

It needs to be at runtime/dynamically as we should not restart machine.



### Have you considered any alternatives?

Exposing using ports is work but I would guess that providing sockets is also a nice alternate way of exposing communication from the host to the containers.

### Additional context

N/A

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I took a look at the extension SDK/contract linked and they are effectively running these extensions in a container, then publishing a unix socket via a volume mount, and proxying from there to a socket on the host. So conceptually the lifecycle is tied more to the container than to the host. In concept, it would be nice to have a container specification mechanism where you could specify a unix socket mapping much like you can specify a port mapping today (e.g. podman run --publish-unix /foo/bar.sock).  While today volume is used for this, it really just falls out as a side-effect (and of course only works when you are on the same host system sharing the same filesystem and the same kernel).  

We could alternatively implement this at the machine / host level as the issue suggests, although if we ever thought this would eventually go the container direction it might be worth making it a private/hidden/undocumented API. It's probably simplest for such a capability to remain transient (bindings added ad-hoc) instead of persisted, so that we dont have to worry about clean-up of dead sockets (no container running)

We have a lot of the pieces already available for this from a machine perspective. Gvproxy already supports establishing a unix-forward much in the same way as a port forward: https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/blob/main/pkg/services/forwarder/ports.go#L93 

On the Windows/WSL side there would need to be some extra work involved to pull in these capabilities.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@benoitf @n1hility Where should we go with this issue?
